===== Castillo2023_bioRxiv_Xenopus+Osteoblast+Enhancers =====
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
1 
 
Title 
1 
Architecture and evolutionary conservation of Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific regulatory 
2 
regions shed light on bone diseases and early skeletal evolution 
3 
 
4 
Short title 
5 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
6 
 
7 
Authors 
8 
Héctor Castillo(1, *), Francisco Godoy(1), Clément Gilbert(2), Felipe Aguilera(1), Salvatore Spicuglia(3), 
9 
Sylvain Marcellini(1,*) 
10 
 
11 
Affiliations 
12 
1 Group for the Study of Developmental Processes (GDeP), School of Biological Sciences, University 
13 
of Concepción, Chile 
14 
2 IDEEV, EGCE, Université Paris Saclay, CNRS, IRD, 12 route 128, 91190, Gif sur Yvette, France 
15 
3 Aix-Marseille University, INSERM, TAGC, UMR 1090, Marseille, France 
16 
* Correspondence to HC, hecastillo@udec.cl or SM, smarcellini@udec.cl 
17 
 
18 
19 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission. 
The copyright holder for this preprint
this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
2 
 
Abstract 
20 
Understanding the genetic mechanisms underpinning the differentiation of osteoblasts, the bone 
21 
producing cells, has far reaching implications for skeletal diseases and evolution. To this end, it is 
22 
crucial to characterize osteoblastic regulatory landscape in a diverse array of distantly-related 
23 
vertebrate species. By comparing of the ATAC-seq profile of Xenopus tropicalis (Xt) osteoblasts to 
24 
liver, heart and lung control tissues, we identified 524 promoters and 6,750 distal regions whose 
25 
chromatin is specifically open in osteoblasts. Nucleotide composition, Gene Ontology, and RNA-Seq 
26 
confirmed that the identified elements correspond to bona fide osteogenic transcriptional enhancers, 
27 
and TFBS enrichment revealed a well-conserved regulatory logic with mammals. Amongst the 357 Xt 
28 
osteoblast-specific enhancers aligning to homologous human loci, 127 map to regions annotated as 
29 
enhancers. Phenotype predictions based on the genes neighbouring these conserved enhancers are 
30 
tightly related to impaired skeletal development. In addition, six conserved enhancers are located at 
31 
loci associated to craniosynostosis (mx2, tcf12), osteopoikilosis (lemd3), osteopenia (gorab), skeletal 
32 
dysplasia (flnb) and craniofacial abnormalities (gpc4). From an evolutionary perspective, the elephant 
33 
shark genome aligns to 53 Xt osteoblast-specific enhancers that are also conserved and annotated as 
34 
enhancers in humans, revealing an ancestral osteogenic role for the ATOH8, IRX3, NFAT, NFIB and 
35 
MEF2C transcription factors, as well as for the FGF, IHH and BMP/TGFb signalling pathways. As 
36 
the absence of bone in sharks is a derived feature, we propose that, in this lineage, the osteogenic 
37 
regulatory network has been maintained for its function in odontoblasts. Our data argues in favour of 
38 
a common origin for dentine and bone, and provides a glimpse into the key regulatory elements and 
39 
upstream activators that drove the formation of an ancient type of mineralized tissue in the vertebrates 
40 
that inhabited the oceans more than 460 million years ago. 
41 
 
42 
Keywords: Xenopus tropicalis, Osteoblasts, ATAC-Seq, Regulatory regions, Bone diseases, Skeletal 
43 
Evolution 
44 
45 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission. 
The copyright holder for this preprint
this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
3 
 
Author Summary 
46 
 
During animal embryogenesis, distinct type of tissues are formed and 
47 
assembled, resulting in an integrated, functional organism. During this process, cells must make 
48 
important decisions, which largely rely on an accurate use of their genetic material. Here, we have 
49 
studied how the genome “knows” that it must participate to the formation of the bone tissue in a frog 
50 
animal model. We therefore identified important genomic regions that are involved in driving the 
51 
expression of genes involved in the formation of a mineralized skeleton. On the one hand, we show 
52 
that some of these regions are also present in humans, and, therefore, skeletal pathologies could be 
53 
studied in the frog model at a genetic level. On the other hand, we also identify regions that are present 
54 
in the genome of a shark, which allows us to propose an evolutionary framework for the early 
55 
evolutionary origin of the vertebrate skeleton. 
56 
 
57 
Introduction 
58 
One daunting challenge of the post-genomic era is to bridge the gap between genotype and 
59 
phenotype to better understand developmental processes, evolutionary trajectories, and the etiology of 
60 
complex diseases [1, 2]. Cracking open the genome’s regulatory code involves understanding how 
61 
distinct cell lineages acquire their specific characteristics through the progressive activation of 
62 
regulatory enhancers and transcriptional programs [3, 4]. In this respect, a major effort has been made 
63 
in identifying and classifying regulatory elements at a genome-wide scale. For instance, more than a 
64 
decade ago, the advent of ChIP-Seq technology allowed a series of pioneering chromatin profiling 
65 
experiments to be performed in flies and mammals [5-8]. More recently, the ATAC-Seq technology 
66 
has provided straightforward access to the repertoire of open chromatin regions genome-wide, which 
67 
are often associated with regulatory potential [9]. Hence, ATAC-Seq has been used to generate open 
68 
chromatin atlases of diverse mammalian tissues [10-18]. The fact that a fraction of distant enhancers 
69 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission. 
The copyright holder for this preprint
this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
4 
 
is rapidly evolving, and cannot readily be identified by sequence similarity searches, emphasizes the 
70 
importance of generating regulatory element databases in a wide variety of species [16]. 
71 
With its sequenced and assembled genome, the amphibian Xenopus tropicalis (Xt) represents a 
72 
powerful model organism to improve our understanding of human genetic disorders [19-25]. Recently, 
73 
ATAC-Seq has been performed in this species to explore gene regulatory networks involved in early 
74 
development [26, 27] and tail regeneration [28]. In Xt, osteogenesis follows a conserved pattern with 
75 
mammals, as mesenchymal precursors progressively differentiate into osteoblasts, the bone producing 
76 
cells [29, 30]. However, amphibians have never been employed to examine the osteoblastic regulatory 
77 
landscape, which has so far only been studied in great depth in mammals [31-35]. In mouse, a 
78 
combination of genome wide approaches, knock-out animals, and reporter experiments, has revealed 
79 
how signalling pathways and transcription factors cooperate at enhancers to drive ossification in a 
80 
well-controlled spatio-temporal fashion [35-46]. In parallel, GWAS studies have allowed the 
81 
identification of polymorphism associated with skeletal diseases [47-49]. Identifying bone-specific 
82 
regulatory elements and upstream activators in Xenopus tropicalis, and, more particularly, those 
83 
components that are conserved with humans, would represent a valuable resource for improving our 
84 
understanding of bone development and disease in this amphibian organism which is readily amenable 
85 
to transgenesis and site-directed mutagenesis [19, 21, 23, 24]. 
86 
In addition to its biomedical relevance, the bone tissue is particularly fascinating from an 
87 
evolutionary perspective, as it represents a major innovation of the vertebrate group by fulfilling 
88 
diverse functions related to body protection and mineral reserves [50]. Indeed, detailed analyses of the 
89 
dermal skeleton from fossils from the Silurian-Devonian period (443-360 Mya) have shown that bone 
90 
emerged long before the radiation of the jawed vertebrates whose extant representative include both 
91 
the chondrichtyans (e.g. elephant shark, sharks and rays) and the osteichthyans (e.g. coelacanth, 
92 
lungfish, gar, teleost fish, amphibian and amniote groups) [50, 51]. One fruitful strategy to explore the 
93 
early origin of the osteoblastic cell type has been to compare the expression of genes coding for bone 
94 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission.

this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
5 
 
matrix components between jawed vertebrate representatives [30, 52-55]. While the evolutionary 
95 
origin of the osteoblastic gene regulatory network has attracted much attention in the literature [56-
96 
58], it still lacks formal experimental assessment, and the nature of the ancestral osteogenic regulatory 
97 
elements and of their upstream input remains unknown. Solving this issue is instrumental to understand 
98 
the origin of the osteoblast, and, more broadly, to shed light on the genetic mechanisms involved in 
99 
the evolution of new animal cell types [59]. 
100 
Here, we compared the ATAC-Seq profile of Xenopus tropicalis native osteoblastic cells to a 
101 
variety of non-mineralizing control tissues such as larval heart, liver and lung. Taken together, our data 
102 
allows us to (i) describe the architecture of the Xt osteoblast-specific promoters and enhancers, (ii) 
103 
identify distal enhancers conserved with human genes involved in skeletal diseases, and (iii) probe the 
104 
evolutionary origin of the regulatory network involved in the emergence of the vertebrate mineralized 
105 
skeleton. 
106 
 
107 
Results 
108 
Identification of osteoblast, liver, heart and lung open chromatin regions in Xenopus tropicalis 
109 
To characterize the nucleosome-free regions (NFRs) of Xt osteoblastic cells, we worked with 
110 
freshly extracted, uncultured, primary osteoblasts obtained following a previously established protocol 
111 
[30]. Our analysis also included larval liver, heart and lung as soft tissues that served as useful controls 
112 
to discriminate between ubiquitous and osteoblast-specific regulatory regions. The osteoblastic 
113 
ATAC-Seq experiments showed the expected pattern of fragment size distribution, with discrete peaks 
114 
corresponding to the NFR, monucleosome and dinucleosome fractions (Fig 1A). By focusing on 
115 
annotated Xt transcription start sites (TSSs), we found that the ATAC-Seq NFR and monucleosome 
116 
signals form two distinct clusters. The first cluster is composed of 5,949 promoters harbouring a robust 
117 
NFR located immediately upstream of the TSS and flanked by two well-positioned nucleosomes (Fig 
118 
1B, left panel), whereas the second cluster contains 16,947 promoters showing weak NFR and diffuse 
119 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission.

Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
6 
 
mononucleosome signals (Fig 1B, right panel). Similar situations were observed for the liver, heart 
120 
and lung tissues (S1 Data). After performing peak calling using the NFR signal, peaks for the four 
121 
tissues were merged into a standard peak list, the number of raw reads mapped to each standard peak 
122 
was counted, and Pearson’s correlations were calculated, depicting a high correlation between the two 
123 
replicates of each sample (Fig 1C). We next examined the distribution of peaks with respect to gene 
124 
bodies (Fig 2). When considering all the peaks from each tissue (Fig 2A), we found a relatively 
125 
consistent pattern, with 26-39% of peaks located at TSSs, 26-34% of intronic peaks, 24-29% of 
126 
intergenic peaks, and a minority of peaks falling into exons and transcription termination sites (TTSs). 
127 
Calculating the distance of all the non-TSS peaks from each tissue to their closest TSS also revealed a 
128 
consistent distribution pattern (Fig 2B). The osteoblastic non-TSS NFRs were located slightly more 
129 
distally than the other samples, with 32% of the peaks included within 5kb of the closest annotated 
130 
promoter, and 53% of the peaks located further away than 10kb of the closest annotated promoter (Fig 
131 
2B). 
132 
Globally, the Pearson correlation coefficient was much higher for TSS than non-TSS peaks 
133 
(Compare Fig 3A and B). This finding is consistent with previous studies showing that, between 
134 
distinct cell types, histone marks are largely invariable at promoters while they display highly context-
135 
dependent patterns at enhancers [6, 7]. Hierarchical clustering showed that the landscape of the NFRs 
136 
from heart and lung are more similar to each other than to osteoblasts or liver, which is true both for 
137 
TSS and non-TSS regions (Fig 3A and B). By contrast, the global distribution of the osteoblastic NFRs 
138 
was the most distinct from the any of the three other assayed tissues (Fig 3A and B). 
139 
 
140 
Characterization of tissue-specific and ubiquitous nucleosome-free regions in Xenopus tropicalis 
141 
We reasoned that subtracting the osteoblastic NFRs with data from a variety of non-mineralized 
142 
control tissues, such as liver, heart and lung, would lead to a substantial enrichment in specific NFRs 
143 
closely related to the activation of genes involved in osteogenesis and bone matrix deposition. To this 
144

Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
7 
 
end, we clustered of all the peaks from the four samples and identified ubiquitous and tissue-specific 
145 
and TSS and non-TSS peaks (Fig 3C, S2 Data). To illustrate that our procedure identifies osteoblast-
146 
specific peaks, figure 3D-D´´´ shows snapshots of the NFR signal at loci of genes known to be involved 
147 
in bone development and evolution such as runx2, sparc, panx3 and fam20c [60-64]. Our approach 
148 
also reveals the presence of tissue-specific peaks for the liver (Fig3E), heart (Fig 3F) and lung (Fig 
149 
3G), as well as ubiquitous peaks that display NFRs in all tissues (Fig 3H).  
150 
Figure 2A´ shows that, with respect to their genomic locations, tissue-specific peaks are mainly 
151 
located in introns (34-55%) and intergenic regions (30-43%), while ubiquitous peaks are 
152 
predominantly found at promoters (41%). When considering only the non-TSS regions, tissue-specific 
153 
peaks are located much more distally than ubiquitous peaks (Fig 2B´). We found a strong correlation 
154 
between the NFR and mononucleosome signals around tissue-specific and ubiquitous TSS and non-
155 
TSS peaks, meaning that open chromatin regions are consistently flanked by two well-positioned 
156 
lateral nucleosomes (S3 Data). 
157 
Gene Ontology analyses on genes whose promoters are most-closely located to the identified 
158 
non-TSS peaks revealed biological processes and molecular functions that are highly consistent with 
159 
each category of tissue-specific or ubiquitous peaks (Fig 4). Hence, osteoblast-specific regions (Fig 
160 
4A) are enriched in skeletogenic genes involved in collagen matrix deposition, osteoblast 
161 
differentiation, as well as TGFbeta and integrin-mediated signaling pathways that are known to 
162 
positively control osteogenesis [65-67]. By contrast, liver-specific regions are associated with genes 
163 
related to the immune system and macrophage biology, while heart-specific regions are associated 
164 
with erythrocytes and blood vessels and, to a lesser extent, with fibroblasts and cardioblasts (Fig 4B 
165 
and C). These results suggest that our tissue-dissociation procedure enriched the liver and heart 
166 
samples in white and red blood cells, respectively, and is consistent with the fact that the larval liver 
167 
is an important site of myelopoiesis in Xenopus [68]. Finally, ubiquitous non-TSS peaks are located 
168 
close to genes involved in fundamental cell biology processes, including transcription, cell cycle, 
169

170 
results were obtained with lung-specific non-TSS ATAC-Seq peaks, nor with any category of TSS 
171 
(data not shown). 
172 
We conclude that our comparative ATAC-Seq experiment allowed the accurate identification 
173 
of distal non-TSS NFR regions that display key features expected for transcriptional enhancers because 
174 
they are found in the vicinity of osteogenic genes and are located much more distally than ubiquitous 
175 
non-TSS peaks. 
176 
 
177 
Sequence features of tissue-specific and ubiquitous open chromatin regions in Xenopus tropicalis 
178 
To further understand the nature of the identified open chromatin regions, we focused our 
179 
analysis on their underlying sequence features. We first examined the CpG content because this 
180 
dinucleotide has been associated with regulatory regions [69-71]. We found that all TSS and non-TSS 
181 
regions display a central CpG enrichment (Fig 5A). In particular, ubiquitous and lung-specific regions 
182 
display the strongest CpG enrichment in TSS (around 5% CpG) and non-TSS (around 2.5% CpG) 
183 
regions (Fig 5A). CpG enrichment is the weakest for liver-specific TSS regions and for heart-specific 
184 
and liver-specific non-TSS regions, where the peak in CpG frequency just reached random levels and 
185 
was largely due to lateral depletion in this dinucleotide (asterisks in Fig 5A). We separately examined 
186 
the frequency in the C and G nucleotides and found that the enrichment is always higher at TSS than 
187 
at non-TSS regions (Fig 5B). In addition, our analysis revealed a higher C and G nucleotide frequency 
188 
for ubiquitous and lung specific TSS (around 30%) and non-TSS (around 26%) regions (Fig 5B). A 
189 
strong GC-skew phenomenon is evident only for ubiquitous TSS (and, to a lesser extent, also for the 
190 
lung tissue, see arrowheads in Fig 5B), which is consistent with the association of GC-skewing with 
191 
non-methylated, highly expressed, promoters [72]. Finally, a lateral depletion in C and G frequency is 
192 
also observed for the osteoblast-specific, heart-specific and liver-specific non-TSS regions (asterisks 
193 
in Fig 5B). While the reason for lateral depletion and central enrichment of C, G and CpG is unclear, 
194 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission.

Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
9 
 
we note that the binding of the CEBPA transcription factor is related to a similar CpG pattern in the 
195 
macaque and dog genomes [73]. 
196 
We then analyzed each type of TSS and non-TSS region for transcription factor binding site 
197 
(TFBS) enrichment (Fig 5C and S4 Data). A detailed description of these findings is shown in S5 Data, 
198 
and, for the sake of clarity, we will focus here on the most crucial aspects of TFBS enrichment found 
199 
at ubiquitous and osteoblast-specific NFR. For instance, ETS, SP and E2F6 have been reported to 
200 
function at ubiquitous and/or highly transcribed promoters [74-77], which is consistent with an 
201 
enrichment for their TFBS in ubiquitous TSS in our dataset (Fig 5C, top panel). Interestingly, 
202 
ubiquitous non-TSS regions are enriched in TFBSs for CTCF and MAZ which have been shown to 
203 
bind and collaborate at insulators and chromatin borders [78, 79]. By contrast, osteoblast-specific TSS 
204 
regions are enriched for SMAD TFBS, in agreement with an important role of the BMP pathway in 
205 
this cell type [38]. Finally, osteoblast-specific non-TSS regions are enriched in TFBS of many well-
206 
known osteogenic regulators in mammals (Fig 5C, bottom panel, S5 Data), including the AP-1 member 
207 
FRA1 [39], TEAD [40], RUNX2 [35], NFIC [41], TWIST1/2 [42] and NFAT [43]. 
208 
Taken together, the analyzed sequence features support the idea that we accurately identified 
209 
each type of TSS and non-TSS regulatory region. Hence, we expect ubiquitous non-TSS regions to 
210 
contain a mixture of ubiquitous enhancers and insulators, while the osteoblast-specific non-TSS dataset 
211 
might be highly enriched in osteogenic enhancers. 
212 
 
213 
The expression strength of genes associated with tissue-specific open chromatin regions is 
214 
context-dependent 
215 
To obtain evidence that the identified regions contain active promoters and enhancers involved 
216 
in transcriptional activation, we assessed whether the NFR presence correlates with gene expression 
217 
in osteoblasts and larval liver. We chose these two samples because they contain the highest number 
218 
of identified regions of interest (Fig 3C) and display the most specific NFR signal (S3 Data). In 
219 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission.

https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543

Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
10 
 
addition, they differ in their CpG and C/G profiles (Fig 5A and B) and are enriched in distinct sets of 
220 
TFBSs (Fig 5C, and S4, S5 Data), which makes the comparison informative from a sequence 
221 
perspective. Hence, RNA-Seq was performed for osteoblasts and larval liver, and gene expression 
222 
profiles were compared between the two cellular contexts (Fig 6). For TSS (Fig 6A, lines 1-6), we 
223 
exclusively used genes whose promoter overlaps with a tissue-specific or ubiquitous peak. We found 
224 
that genes associated with osteoblast-specific TSS peaks are expressed more robustly in osteoblasts 
225 
than in the liver (Fig 6A, lines 1 and 2). Conversely, genes associated with liver-specific TSS peaks 
226 
are expressed more strongly in the liver than in osteoblasts (Fig 6A, lines 3 and 4). Finally, genes 
227 
associated with ubiquitously opened TSS are expressed at similar levels in both tissues (Fig 6A, lines 
228 
5 and 6). For non-TSS regions (Fig 6A, lines 7-12), we exclusively used genes whose promoter was 
229 
located closest to a tissue-specific or ubiquitous peak. Although the nearest gene is not necessarily the 
230 
target of the identified enhancer, we found that, like for TSS, the non-TSS peaks display a strong 
231 
tissue-dependent behavior. Hence, genes associated with osteoblast-specific non-TSS peaks are 
232 
expressed more strongly in osteoblasts than in the liver (Fig 6A, lines 7 and 8). The opposite is true 
233 
for liver-specific non-TSS peaks (Fig 6A, lines 9 and 10). Finally, only marginal expression difference 
234 
(p = 0.026) was observed, between each sample, for genes whose promoter is located close to a 
235 
ubiquitous non-TSS peak (Fig 6A, lines 11 and 12). Taken together, these data strongly support the 
236 
notion that we successfully identified TSS and non-TSS NFR associated with transcriptionally active 
237 
genes, and, most importantly, that the set of osteoblast-specific non-TSS NFR corresponds to bona 
238 
fide osteoblastic enhancers.

Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions

Conservation degree of X. tropicalis tissue-specific and ubiquitous open chromatin regions 
242 
among chordates 
243 
To explore the biomedical and evolutionary relevance of our dataset, we assessed the degree 
244 
of conservation of the identified NFRs with humans as well as with six other species belonging to the 
245 
chordate phylum (Fig 7 and S6 Data). For this purpose, we used Xt NFRs as queries to perform a series 
246 
of similarity searches using BLASTN against the genomes of the clawed frog Xenopus laevis [a pipidae 
247 
frog which diverged from Xt 48 Mya, 80], of two amniote species [human and chick, which diverged 
248 
from Xt around 351-333 Mya, 81], of the zebrafish Danio rerio [a teleost fish which diverged from Xt 
249 
around 458-449 Mya, 81], of the elephant shark Callorhinchus milii [a chondrichthyan showing 
250 
mineralized skeletal elements, which diverged from Xt shortly before the split with the teleost fish 
251 
lineage, 61, 81, 82, 83], of the lamprey Petromyzon marinus [a cyclostome, devoid of any type of 
252 
mineralized tissues, which diverged from Xt around 500 Mya, 50, 84], and of the amphioxus 
253 
Branchiostoma floridae [a non-vertebrate cephalochordate species which diverged from Xt around 550 
254 
Mya, 50, 85]. For the sake of comparison, we also performed BLASTN analyses using as queries the 
255 
lung-specific, heart-specific, liver-specific and ubiquitous regulatory elements, and also conducted the 
256 
same analysis with two sets of randomly selected regions of the Xt genome whose length matches the 
257 
average length of the NFR at TSS and non-TSS (S6 Data). We computed an enrichment factor defined 
258 
as the percentage of hits obtained with any type of NFR divided by the percentage of hits obtained 
259 
with random regions. This analysis showed that, globally, the osteoblast-specific and lung-specific 
260 
NFR are well-conserved (with various enrichment factor superior to 2), the liver-specific NFR are 
261 
particularly divergent (with various enrichment factor inferior to 0.5), and the heart and ubiquitous 
262 
NFR exhibit an intermediate conservation level, often yielding an enrichment factor close to 1 (Fig 7). 
263 
Taken together, this data is consistent with the notion that the conservation degree of enhancer 
264 
elements can vary greatly depending on their tissue-specificity [86]. 
265 
 
266 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission.

Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
12 
 
Identification osteoblast-specific regulatory regions relevant for understanding human 
267 
osteogenesis and skeletal pathologies. 
268 
Of a total of 524 osteoblast-specific TSS peaks, 48 align to the promoter of their corresponding 
269 
homologous gene in the human genome (Fig 7, S6 and S7 Data), representing a 4.8 fold increase with 
270 
respect to random. By performing a literature search, we found that 19 of these genes have been 
271 
associated to the osteogenic and skeletogenic processes (S7 Data), including genes coding for critical 
272 
osteogenic transcription factors such as RUNX2 and SIX2 [64, 87], and for important bone matrix 
273 
components such as ADAM12 and TYPE III COLLAGEN [88, 89]. 
274 
Amongst the 6,779 osteoblast-specific non-TSS peaks, 370 align to the human genome and, 
275 
among these, 127 match to human regions annotated as enhancers, representing a 16-fold enrichment 
276 
factor with respect to control (Fig 7, S6 and S8 Data). We found that synteny was conserved between 
277 
Xt and human for 357 NFR (i.e. in 96.5% of the cases, S8 Data). None of the 13 osteoblast-specific 
278 
non-TSS peaks that did not show conserved synteny were annotated as enhancers in humans, 
279 
suggesting that they either represent false positives from our experiment, or that they act as long-range 
280 
enhancers. We compared TFBS enrichment profile between the set of osteoblast-specific non-TSS 
281 
regions that align to the human genome, and those that lack conservation, and found that binding sites 
282 
for the previously identified osteogenic transcription factors (AP-1, NFAT, NFIC, RUNX, TWIST, 
283 
TEAD, see Fig 5 and Data S4, S5) were enriched in both groups (S9 Data). One marked difference, 
284 
however, was observed for the CTCF binding site that appears to be highly enriched only in non-
285 
conserved NFR (S9 Data). Taken together, this data suggests that the conserved regions correspond 
286 
almost exclusively to transcriptional enhancers, while the non-conserved regions contain a 
287 
combination of enhancers and insulators. 
288 
By applying Great on the 370 osteoblast-specific non-TSS NFR that are conserved between Xt 
289 
and human, we obtained a strong association for human phenotypes related to anomalous skeletal 
290 
development (S10 Data). Finally, all the genes located at the loci of these conserved regions were 
291 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission.

searched in a database of skeletal diseases [49], leading to the identification of putative osteogenic 
292 
enhancers for flnb, gorab, gpc4, lemd3, msx2 and tcf12 (S11 Data). Snapshots of ATAC-Seq and RNA-
293 
Seq profiles at the lemd3 (associated to the Buschke-Ollendorff syndrome), msx2 (associated to 
294 
Craniosynostosis 2), tcf12 (associated to Craniosynostosis 3) and gpc4 (associated to Keipert 
295 
syndrome) loci are shown in Fig 8A. 
296 
 
297 
Comparison of the conserved X. troplicalis osteoblast-specific NFR with jawed vertebrates 
298 
Blasting the Xt osteoblast-specific TSS against the elephant shark genome recovered twice as 
299 
many hits than expected by chance, leading to the identification of 15 promoters that are conserved 
300 
between Xt, the elephant shark, chick and human (S12 Data). Of these, 7 promoters belong to genes 
301 
associated with an osteogenic function in mammals, such as the emblematic Runx2 gene [see S12 Data 
302 
and Ref 64]. We identified 173 osteoblast-specific non-TSS NFR that align to the elephant shark 
303 
genome, which only corresponds to an enrichment factor of 1.0 (S6 Data and Fig 7). Therefore, in an 
304 
attempt to identify bona fide osteogenic enhancers that might have evolved at the base of the jawed 
305 
vertebrates, we focussed our analysis on osteoblast-specific non-TSS sequences that are conserved 
306 
between Xt and (i) elephant shark, (ii) chick, (iii) human, and (iv) that are also annotated as enhancers 
307 
in the human genome. As shown in S13 Data, 53 open chromatin regions fulfil all these conditions, all 
308 
of which displaying a conserved synteny between Xt and elephant shark. The location of these regions 
309 
is biased towards transcription factors (18 genes, 12 of which having been reported to play a role during 
310 
mammalian osteogenesis), and signalling pathway components (10 genes), including actors of the Fgf, 
311 
Ihh, and BMP/TGFb signalling pathways which play instrumental roles during mammalian 
312 
osteogenesis (S13 Data). Examples of ancient bone-specific enhancers located at the atoh8, gli3, 
313 
smad1 and spry1 loci are shown in Fig 8B.

14 
 
Discussion 
317 
On the nature of the open chromatin regions in non-mineralizing tissues 
318 
As native osteoblasts were obtained using a previously established extraction protocol [30], it 
319 
is not surprising that this sample provides the most biologically consistent signal regarding Gene 
320 
Ontology (Fig 4), TFBS enrichment (Fig 5, S4 and S5 Data), and the known osteogenic function of 
321 
the mammalian genes associated with conserved TSS and non-TSS peaks (S7, S10, S11, S12 and S13 
322 
Data). By contrast, the cellular composition of the non-mineralizing tissues is less controlled, and GO 
323 
and TFBS data suggest that the heart and liver mostly contain red and white blood cells, respectively, 
324 
in agreement with the function of these two organs (Fig 4 and 5, and S5 Data). By far, the lung is the 
325 
most puzzling tissue in this study as it exhibits unique characteristics that are not straightforward to 
326 
interpret. This is because the lung-specific NFRs are very few (Fig 3), their nucleotide signature 
327 
resembles that of ubiquitous elements (Fig 5), yet their patterns of conservation with other genomes 
328 
are amongst the highest (Fig 7). In addition, the lung-specific non-TSS NFR are enriched in TFBS for 
329 
HNRNP-K [a transcription factor involved in stem cell regulation and lung regeneration, see Refs 90, 
330 
91], and are located in the vicinity of genes known to play a role in stem cells of the lung and other 
331 
tissues (S14 Data). We therefore propose that our dissociation protocol somehow led to a partial 
332 
enrichment in lung pluripotent stem cells [92]. In conclusion, while the exact cellular composition of 
333 
the three non-mineralizing control tissues remains unknown, the body of data provided here shows that 
334 
they successfully served the purpose of identifying ubiquitous as well as osteoblast-specific TSS and 
335 
non-TSS NFRs. 
336 
 
337 
Major differences in the architecture of tissue-specific and ubiquitous NFR in Xenopus tropicalis 
338 
 
We found that ubiquitous and tissue-specific NFR differ in many aspects, in agreement with 
339 
data obtained in mammals. For instance, when compared to tissue-specific promoters, Xt ubiquitous 
340 
promoters are clearly GC-skewed [72], they harbour a higher CpG content and control genes related 
341 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission. 
The copyright holder for this preprint
this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
15 
 
to basic cellular functions [74, 93, 94], and they are enriched in TFBS for the ETS, SP and E2F6 
342 
transcription factors known to bind the TSS of broadly expressed genes [74-77]. In addition, only the 
343 
ubiquitous non-TSS NFRs are enriched in both CTCF and MAZ TFBS, suggesting that many of them 
344 
act as insulators [78, 79]. The osteoblast-specific non-TSS regions display some CTCF enrichment, 
345 
which might correspond to tissue-specific insulators [95]. The fact that these CTCF sites are 
346 
predominantly enriched in non-conserved regions (S9 Data) suggests that bone-specific insulators are 
347 
evolutionarily more labile than enhancers, and, therefore, that osteoblast-specific chromatin domains 
348 
might show substantial variation between frogs and humans. Finally, while the ubiquitous non-TSS 
349 
NFR are mostly proximal, the distal location of Xt tissue-specific enhancers resembles the distribution 
350 
of developmental enhancers [96], consistently with the idea that more complex regulatory architectures 
351 
are required to generate specific expression patterns. 
352 
The presence of the TATA-box consensus in ubiquitous Xt promoters (Fig 5 and S4, S5 Data) 
353 
clearly represents the most divergent feature with mammals where the presence of a TATA-box is 
354 
associated with genes showing tissue-specific expression patterns [69]. The TATA-box being the only 
355 
promoter motif conserved in all eukaryotic genomes examined to date, it is striking that it contributes 
356 
to different promoter classes in amphibians and mammals. This shift in the use of the TATA-box motif 
357 
might have been driven by the evolution of differences in nucleotide composition in these vertebrate 
358 
lineages [97]. 
359 
 
360 
Global conservation of regulatory regions 
361 
Our comparative analysis (Fig 7) revealed a series of noticeable features that are consistent 
362 
with a body of published literature on other vertebrate species. First, we find that TSS regions are 
363 
usually better conserved than non-TSS regions (as an example, human TSS regions always yield a 
364 
higher percentage of hits and a higher enrichment factors than human non-TSS regions, compare the 
365 
relative position of grey circles and grey squares on Fig 7), a situation that has also been observed 
366 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission. 
The copyright holder for this preprint
this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
16 
 
between mammalian species having diverged 180 Mya [98]. Second, the identified Xt regulatory 
367 
elements (as well as the random regions) align approximately twice as much to the Xenopus laevis L 
368 
subgenome than to the S subgenome. This observation is consistent with the fact that the S subgenome 
369 
is particularly prone to deletions and has significantly diverged [80]. Third, Danio rerio displays one 
370 
of the most divergent genomes, often showing enrichment values inferior to those obtained when 
371 
blasting the genomes of most remotely related species such as the elephant shark, lamprey, or even 
372 
amphioxus. In this respect, it is worth mentioning that the genome of the gar (a non-teleost ray-finned 
373 
fish) is much better conserved with tetrapods than the Danio rerio genome [99]. Fourth, some NFR 
374 
display enrichment scores that are lower than expected by chance, a situation which is particularly 
375 
evident when blasting the liver-specific NFR on the lamprey genome (Fig 8, bottom panel). If we 
376 
consider that the liver sample led to the detection of Xt lymphocytic promoters and enhancers (Figs 4 
377 
and 5, S5 Data), the overall low conservation degree we observed might reflect a rapid divergence rate 
378 
for the immune gene regulatory network since its origin at the base of the vertebrate more than 500 
379 
million years ago [100]. This idea is consistent with the absence of conservation for a macrophage 
380 
enhancer at the CSF1R locus [101]. Fifth, the set of lung-specific enhancers aligns noticeably well to 
381 
the amphioxus genome (2 fold enrichment score, see Fig 7 and S6 Data), considering that 
382 
approximately 550 My separate this species from Xt. As amphioxus lacks lungs, this observation might 
383 
reflect a conservation of enhancers related to stem cell function, as we have proposed above. This 
384 
hypothesis supported by a study examining the regeneration process in amphioxus [102]. 
385 
 
386 
A well-conserved regulatory logic for tetrapod osteoblastic enhancers 
387 
Remarkably, many of the TFBS enriched in the set of Xt osteoblast-specific NFR are bound by 
388 
key osteogenic regulators in mammals such as SMAD1 at TSS, and AP-1, TEAD, Runx2, NFIC, 
389 
TWIST1/2 and NFAT at non-TSS regions [35, 38-43], reflecting a highly conserved regulatory logic 
390 
for osteogenesis at the tetrapod level. While the TWIST1 and 2 proteins have mainly been reported to 
391 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission. 
The copyright holder for this preprint
this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
17 
 
delay the differentiation process by binding and inhibiting RUNX2 [42], one study showed that 
392 
TWIST1 also directly repressed the C-ROS-1 promoter, thereby negatively regulating osteogenic 
393 
differentiation [103]. In this respect, the enrichment in TWIST1/2 TFBS at Xt osteogenic enhancers is 
394 
a novel finding and might suggests that TWIST proteins control the onset of osteoblastic differentiation 
395 
through redundant repressive mechanisms that act both at the post-translational and transcriptional 
396 
levels. The only “new player” binding the osteoblast-specific promoters seems to be TFIIIA which 
397 
controls the transcription of ribosomal genes associated with the Pol-III promoter in Xenopus laevis 
398 
[104]. Hence, according to our work, TFIIIA might also regulate Xt Pol-II promoters during 
399 
osteogenesis. 
400 
By examining 6,380 enhancers devoid of sequence conservation (S9 Data), we detected TFBS 
401 
enrichment for the AP-1, NFAT, NFIC, RUNX, TWIST and TEAD transcription factors which are 
402 
important regulators of osteogenesis in mammals (S5 and S9 Data). This shows that, during the 350 
403 
millions of years that separate amphibians from the amniote lineage, the osteoblastic enhancers have 
404 
maintained a well-conserved regulatory grammar despite extensive sequence turnover, a phenomenon 
405 
commonly observed in distinct animal groups and biological contexts [105-107]. This observation 
406 
highlights the importance of experimentally characterizing osteoblastic distal enhancers in a variety of 
407 
vertebrate species, as such regulatory elements cannot be exhaustively identified by sequence 
408 
similarity searches. 
409 
 
410 
Identification of new osteogenic enhancers relevant for human biology 
411 
Here, we report 370 non-TSS osteoblast-specific NFRs that are conserved with humans 
412 
(including 127 regions annotated as enhancers in the human genome), that are globally associated with 
413 
a skeletogenic function (S8, S10 Data), and that are enriched in binding sites transcription factors 
414 
known to play a major osteogenic role in mammals (S9 Data). By crossing our data with a recently 
415 
updated nosology of genetic skeletal disorders [49], we identified 7 conserved enhancers at loci of 
416 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission. 
The copyright holder for this preprint
this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
18 
 
genes involved in skeletal pathologies such as lemd3, gpc4, tcf12, trps1, flnb, prrx1 and msx2 (S11 
417 
Data, Fig 8A). Hence, the 370 conserved enhancers reported here could potentially be further studied 
418 
in Xt to investigate the genetic bases of skeletal diseases. 
419 
 
420 
Evolutionary origin of the osteoblast-specific gene regulatory network 
421 
The Xt osteoblastic enhancers are particularly well-conserved (S6 Data, Fig 7), providing a 
422 
unique opportunity to probe the evolutionary origin of the osteoblast-specific gene regulatory network. 
423 
The location of the 53 Xt osteoblast-specific regions that are annotated as enhancers in humans and 
424 
align to the human, chick and elephant shark genomes is biased towards genes encoding transcription 
425 
factors and signalling pathway components (S13 Data). By focusing on genes with known osteoblastic 
426 
roles in mammals, we propose that the ATOH8, BNC2, EBF3, FOXP1 and 2, HMGA2, IRX3, 
427 
MEF2C, NFATC1, NFIB, ZEB2 and ZFHX4 transcription factors are ancient players involved in 
428 
skeletal mineralization (S13 Data, Fig 8B). Likewise, enhancers located at the spry1, gli3, ptch1, 
429 
tgfbr3, smad1 and bmp4 loci draw attention to the evolutionary importance of the Ffg, Hh and Bmp 
430 
signalling pathways (S13 Data, Fig 8B). What could possibly be, however, the function of osteoblastic 
431 
enhancers in a representative species of the cartilaginous fish, a group that lacks bone tissue and 
432 
osteoblasts? This question is better answered if we first consider that the absence of bone is a secondary 
433 
loss, and that cartilaginous fish evolved from a species that already possessed an ancestral form of 
434 
bony tissue, similar to the plates covering the body of placoderms and osteostracan fossils [50, 51]. 
435 
Second, it is important to note that bone and dentine share many similarities at the tissular, cellular and 
436 
genetic levels [52, 61, 108, 109]. Here, we propose that osteoblasts and odontoblasts evolved from 
437 
common a proto-skeletal cell that relied on the aforementioned transcription factors and signalling 
438 
pathways. After the bone tissue loss in the chondrichthyan lineage, this ancestral regulatory network 
439 
was maintained for its function in odontoblasts. According to this hypothesis, the genetic toolkit we 
440 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission.

;

Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
19 
 
uncovered might well have evolved long before the cartilaginous/bony fish split, around 460 million 
441 
years ago, in the first cell type able to mineralize a rudimentary form of bone tissue. 
442 
 
443 
Materials and methods 
444 
Xenopus tropicalis handling and bioethical considerations  
445 
The Ethics Committee of the University of Concepcion (Concepcion, Chile) approved all experimental 
446 
procedures carried out during this study, which were performed following the guidelines outlined in 
447 
the Biosafety and Bioethics Manual of the National Commission of Scientific and Technological 
448 
Research (CONICYT, Chilean Government). Xenopus tropicalis tadpoles were staged according to 
449 
the Nieuwkoop and Faber developmental table [110] and anesthetized with Tricaine (MS-222, Sigma), 
450 
as recommended [111, 112].  
451 
 
452 
Sample collection and ATAC-Seq library construction 
453 
Tissue disaggregation was carried out adapting the protocol of Bertin et al [30]. Briefly, the 
454 
frontoparietal bone, liver, heart and lung were dissected from euthanatized premetamorphic (NF58) 
455 
tadpoles in PBS 0.6X. Each tissue was transferred to different tubes containing 0.6X HBSS (HyClone). 
456 
The supernatants were discarded and replaced with 400 μL of 0.6X HBSS containing 0.1% collagenase 
457 
P (Roche), and the tubes were gently agitated for 40 mins. The supernatants were discarded and 
458 
replaced with 400 μL of 0.6X HBSS containing 0.1% collagenase P and 0.125% trypsin (Gibco), and 
459 
the tubes were gently agitated for 10 mins. Finally, the supernatants were discarded and replaced with 
460 
400 μL of 0.6X HBSS containing 0.1% collagenase P and 0.125% trypsin, and the tubes were gently 
461 
agitated for 15 mins. This last extraction step was performed four times, and all the supernatants were 
462 
pooled in four tubes (a single tube per sample type) containing 1 mL of 0.6X L-15 (Leibovitz-15, 
463 
HyClone). Cells were subsequently centrifuged at 500g for 7 mins at room temperature. After 
464 
centrifugation, the supernatant was removed and the cells were resuspended in 50 μL of cold (4°C) 
465 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission. 
The copyright holder for this preprint
this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
20 
 
0.6X PBS and centrifuged at 500g for 5 mins at 4°C. Cells were resuspended in 50 µL of lysis buffer 
466 
(10mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.4, 10mM NaCl, 3mM MgCl2, 0.1% Igepal), followed by pipetting up and down 
467 
and incubating for 3 mins. A volume of 45 μL of lysed cells was diluted in 1 mL of PBS 0.6X and 
468 
centrifugated at 500g for 10 mins at 4°C. During centrifugation, the remaining 5 μL of lysed cells was 
469 
used to count the nuclei after trypan blue addition. After centrifugation, the supernatants were 
470 
removed, and the nuclei were resuspended in 10 μL of PBS 0.6X. Approximately 80,000–100,000 
471 
nuclei per sample were transferred into fresh tubes and then centrifuged at 500g for 5 mins at 4°C. 
472 
Again, the supernatants were carefully aspirated. Transposition reaction was performed following the 
473 
Buenrostro et al. protocol [113]. The pelleted nuclei were gently resuspended with 50 µl of 
474 
transposition reaction containing 25 µL 2X Tagmentation buffer (Illumina Cat FC-121-1030), 5 µL 
475 
Tn5 transposase (Illumina Cat FC-121-1030) and 22,5 µL nuclease-free H2O and incubated at 37 °C 
476 
for 30 mins. Transposed DNA was purified using a QIAGEN MinElute Reaction Cleanup kit (28204) 
477 
and eluted with 10 μL of elution buffer (10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8). The purified DNA was amplified 
478 
using a reaction mixture containing 2X NEB PCR Master Mix (NEB M0541L), a universal forward 
479 
primer i5, and an indexed reverse primer from the i7 Illumina series, with a PCR protocol of 72°C for 
480 
5 mins, 98°C for 30 s, and then eleven cycles of 98°C for 10 s, 63°C for 30 s,72°C for 1 min, with a 
481 
final step at 72°C for 10 mins and hold at 4°C. PCR products were then cleaned up using AMPure 
482 
beads (Agencourt APure XP, Beckman Coulter, A63880) to remove small DNA fragments according 
483 
to the manual. ATAC-Seq libraries were then analyzed by Fragment Bioanalyzer and sequenced on an 
484 
Illumina NextSeq 500 sequencer at the Transcriptomic and Genomic Marseille Luminy Platform (Aix-
485 
Marseille University, TAGC). 
486 
 
487 
Processing of ATAC-Seq data 
488 
Raw sequencing fastq files were assessed for quality, adapter content and duplication rates with 
489 
FastQC v0.11.9 and trimmed using Cutadapt [114]. Trimmed reads were mapped against the Xenopus 
490 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission. 
The copyright holder for this preprint
this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
21 
 
tropicalis reference genome (v9.1) using Bowtie2 [115] with the --very-sensitive parameter. Mapped 
491 
reads whose quality was inferior to 40 and reads mapped to the mitochondrial genome were filtered 
492 
out with SAMtools [116, 117]. Then, the “MarkDuplicates” function from Picard (Picard Tools, Broad 
493 
Institute, 2018, https://broadinstitute.github.io/picard/) was used to remove duplicate reads and 
494 
estimate library complexity. To obtain the NFR-associated reads, all reads larger than 120 bp were 
495 
eliminated. 
Peak 
calling 
was 
performed 
using 
Genrich 
v0.6.1 
(available 
at 
496 
https://github.com/jsh58/Genrich" \h) including two biological replicates per tissue with the 
497 
parameters -j (ATAC-seq mode) -q 0.05 (FDR adjusted p-value). Peaks for the four tissues were 
498 
merged into a standard peak list. The number of raw reads mapped to each standard peak was counted 
499 
using the multicov function of BedTools v2.29.2 [118]. The edgeR package [119] was used to 
500 
normalize the raw counts by TMM for each sample and the R function cor was used to calculate 
501 
Pearson’s correlation between biological replicates. After the calculation of the scale factor, genome 
502 
browser tracks in bigwig format were generated using deepTools bamCoverage [120]. 
503 
 
504 
NFR and Mononucleosome arrangement 
505 
NucleoATAC with default parameters was applied to infer nucleosome positions for each tissue 
506 
replicate [121]. TSS enrichment, plotting and clustering for NFR and mononucleosome were 
507 
performed by the deepTools functions computeMatrix, plotProfile and plotHeatmap, respectively. 
508 
 
509 
Identification of tissue-specific open chromatin regions 
510 
To quantitatively measure the relative occupancy for each peak, we adopted a strategy based on 
511 
Shannon Entropy to assign a tissue-specificity index to each element [10, 122, 123]. For each peak, 
512 
we defined its relative accessibility in a tissue type i as Ri = Ei/ΣE, where Ei is the TMM value for the 
513 
peak in the tissue i, ΣE is the sum of TMM values in all tissues. The entropy score for each peak across 
514 
tissues can be defined as H = −1 * sum(Ri * log2Ri). An entropy score close to zero indicates the 
515 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission. 
The copyright holder for this preprint
this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543
doi: 
bioRxiv preprint 
Xenopus tropicalis osteoblast-specific open chromatin regions 
22 
 
accessibility of this peak is highly tissue-specific. Based on the distribution of entropy scores, peaks 
516 
displaying scores inferior to 1.2 were selected as tissue-restricted peaks. 
517 
 
518 
Tissue-specific and ubiquitous ATAC peaks analysis 
519 
HOMER v4.11 [124] was used to annotate genomic elements such as TSS and non-TSS (exonic, TTS, 
520 
intergenic and intronic), determine the enrichment of transcription factor binding sites (TFBS), and 
521 
calculate the %GC and %CpG from tissue-specific and ubiquitous peak sequences. Enriched Gene 
522 
Ontology (GO) terms were found with topGO [125]. The p-value of GO analysis was adjusted with 
523 
BH correction and only the top terms for tissue-specific and ubiquitous open chromatin regions were 
524 
plotted. 
525 
To assess whether tissue-specific and ubiquitous peak sequences are conserved in other species, these 
526 
regions were BLASTed against the following genomes using BLASTN (-outfmt 6, -task blastn, -
527 
max_target_seqs 1 parameters): Homo sapiens (hg19), Xenopus laevis (XenLae10.1), Danio rerio 
528 
(denRa11), Gallus gallus (galGal6), Callorhinchus milii (calMil1), Petromyzon marinus (petMar3) 
529 
and Branchiostoma floridae (braFlo1). BLASTN alignments inferior to 100 bp were removed and only 
530 
those alignments with the best score were kept for each region of interest. Finally, peaks BLASTed 
531 
with the hg19 genome were crossed with a list of annotated enhancers in nine different human cell 
532 
lines (Gm12878, H1-hESC, HepG2, HMEC, HSMM, HUVEC, K562, NHEK, NHLF). Synteny 
533 
determination and phenotyping analyzes were performed using GREAT v4 [126, 127]. 
534 
 
535 
Sample collection and RNA-Seq library preparation 
536 
While liver tissue was disaggregated with a plastic pestle in TriReagent (Sigma), frontoparietal bone 
537 
osteoblasts were extracted as described in Bertin et al [30] and then transferred to TriReagent. 
538 
Homogenized samples were incubated for 5 mins at room temperature and centrifuged at 12,000g for 
539 
15 mins at 4ºC. The supernatants were transferred to fresh tubes and 100 µL of BCP (1-bromo-3-
540 
(which was not certified by peer review) is the author/funder. All rights reserved. No reuse allowed without permission.

this version posted September 1, 2023. 
; 
https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.08.30.555543

chloropropane, Sigma) were added. Samples were incubated for 10 mins at room temperature and 
541 
centrifuged at 12,000g for 15 mins at 4ºC. The aqueous phase was transferred to a new Eppendorf tube 
542 
and 125 µL of extraction buffer (1M Tris, 8M LiCl, 0.5M EDTA, 10% SDS) prewarmed at 56ºC and 
543 
10 µL of proteinase K (10 mg/mL) were added and mixed. After 30 minutes of incubation, 250 µL of 
544 
chloroform were added and mixed. The samples were incubated for 10 mins at 56ºC and centrifuged 
545 
at 12,000g for 15 mins at 4ºC. The supernatants were transferred to fresh tubes and 250 µL of high salt 
546 
precipitation solution (1.2 M NaCl, 0.8 M C6H5O7Na3) and 250 µL of isopropanol were added. The 
547 
samples were mixed and incubated overnight at 20ºC. Then, the samples were centrifuged at 12,000g 
548 
for 10 mins at room temperature and the supernatants were discarded. The RNA pellets were washed 
549 
with 300 µL of 70% ethanol, centrifuged at 12,000g for 5 mins at room temperature and finally 
550 
dissolved in DEPC-treated water. The concentration and quality of the extracted RNA were determined 
551 
using a NanoDrop Lite (Thermo). RNA-Seq libraries were constructed at the Transcriptomic and 
552 
Genomic Marseille Luminy Platform (Aix-Marseille University, TAGC) using the TruSeq mRNA 
553 
Library Prep Kit v2 (Illumina; California, USA). Libraries were paired-end sequenced on an Illumina 
554 
NextSeq 500 sequencer. 
555 
 
556 
Processing of RNA-Seq data 
557 
Raw sequencing fastq files quality were assessed with FastQC, trimmed using Cutadapt and mapped 
558 
against the Xenopus tropicalis transcriptome (v9.1) using HISAT2 [128]. Mapped reads whose quality 
559 
was inferior to 40 and those reads mapped to the mitochondrial genome were filtered out with 
560 
SAMtools [116, 117]. The number of raw reads mapped to each transcript was counted using the 
561 
multicov function of BedTools v2.29.2 [118]. The edgeR package [119] was used to normalize the raw 
562 
counts by TMM for both samples and R function cor to calculate Pearson’s correlation between 
563 
biological replicates.

===== Cordero2023_NatCommun_miR9+G-quadruplex+TGFbeta =====
Article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-54740-x
Nuclear microRNA 9 mediates G-quadruplex
formation and 3D genome organization
during TGF-β-induced transcription
Julio Cordero1,2,3
, Guruprasadh Swaminathan
4, Diana G. Rogel-Ayala
3,4,
Karla Rubio3,4,5,6, Adel Elsherbiny1,2, Samina Mahmood7, Witold Szymanski
8,
Johannes Graumann
8, Thomas Braun
9, Stefan Günther
7,9,
Gergana Dobreva
1,2,10 & Guillermo Barreto
3,4
The dynamics of three-dimensional (3D) genome organization are essential to
transcriptional regulation. While enhancers regulate spatiotemporal gene
expression, chromatin looping is a means for enhancer-promoter interactions
yielding cell-type-speciﬁc gene expression. Further, non-canonical DNA sec-
ondary structures, such as G-quadruplexes (G4s), are related to increased gene
expression. However, the role of G4s in promoter-distal regulatory elements,
such as super-enhancers (SE), and in chromatin looping has remained elusive.
Here we show that mature microRNA 9 (miR-9) is enriched at promoters and SE
of genes that are inducible by transforming growth factor beta 1 (TGFB1) sig-
naling. Moreover, we ﬁnd that miR-9 is required for formation of G4s,
promoter-super-enhancer looping and broad domains of the euchromatin
histone mark H3K4me3 at TGFB1-responsive genes. Our study places miR-9 in
the same functional context with G4s and promoter-enhancer interactions
during 3D genome organization and transcriptional activation induced by
TGFB1 signaling, a critical signaling pathway in cancer and ﬁbrosis.
The nuclear genome in eukaryotic cells consists of DNA molecules
packaged into thread-like structures known as chromosomes, which
are built of chromatin. Thus, chromatin is the physiological template
for biological processes in the nucleus of eukaryotic cells. Studying
how chromatin is folded inside the cell nucleus and its dynamic three-
dimensional (3D) structure is essential to understanding these biolo-
gical processes comprising transcription, RNA-splicing, -processing,
-editing, DNA-replication, -recombination, and -repair. The chromatin
is hierarchically organized at different levels including chromosomal
territories, compartments, and self-interacting topologically associat-
ing domains, altogether giving rise to a highly dynamic 3D genome
organization1. Remarkably, the structure of the genome is intrinsically
linked to its function as shown by extensive correlations between
chromatin condensation and related gene transcription. For example,
Received: 13 December 2023
Accepted: 20 November 2024

chromatin shows condensed regions, referred to as heterochromatin
(by convention, transcriptionally “inactive”), and less condensed
regions, referred to as euchromatin (transcriptionally “active”). Tran-
scriptional regulation directly corresponds to the mechanisms of how
chromatin may be structurally arranged rendering it accessible to the
transcription machinery2. These mechanisms regulating chromatin
structure and transcription involve histone modiﬁcations, histone
deposition, nucleosome remodeling, DNA methylation, non-coding
RNAs (ncRNA), and secondary structures of nucleic acids, among
others3–7. In addition, an increasing number of recent publications
based on integrative analysis of multi-omics studies implementing
next-generation sequencing (NGS) technologies, chromosome con-
formation capture-based methods, and super-resolution microscopy
have provided comprehensive and multilevel insights into 3D genome
organization emphasizing its role during transcriptional regulation8.
Chromatin structure alone does not determine the functional
status of a gene, but it effectively enables RNA polymerase II (Poll II)
recruitment to the promoters, as well as binding of transcription fac-
tors, co-activators, co-repressors to DNA sequences that function as
regulatory elements controlling gene expression9. A promoter is a
sequence of DNA to which proteins bind to initiate transcription of
RNA molecules that are usually complementary to the DNA sequence
that is located 3´ of the promoter. On the other hand, enhancers are
relatively short (~100–1000 bp) DNA sequences that are bound by
transcription factors and regulate gene transcription independent of
their distance, location, or orientation relative to their cognate
promoter10. Super-enhancers (SE) have been proposed to be long
genomic domains consisting of clusters of transcriptional enhancers
enriched with histone modiﬁcation markers (such as histone 3 mono-
methylated at lysine 4 or acetylated at lysine 27, H3K4me1 and
H3K27ac respectively), cofactors (such as mediator of RNA polymerase
II transcription subunit 1, MED1, and components of the multimeric
protein complex Cohesin), chromatin modifying proteins (such as E1A
Binding Protein P300, EP300) and cell-type-speciﬁc transcription
factors11–13. One versatile feature of chromatin is its ability to form
loops, mediating long-range interactions in which two distant
sequences of DNA come into close physical proximity. Chromatin
looping has been broadly accepted as a means for enhancer-promoter
interactions14.
In addition to the predominant DNA double-helix structure, there
are different non-canonical DNA secondary structures, including
G-quadruplex (G4), R-loop, H-DNA, Z-DNA, etc. 4. A G4 represents a
stable nucleic acid secondary structure formed by square planes, in
which four guanines located in the same plane are stabilized by a
monovalent cation15. While the early work on G4s mainly focused on
their roles in telomeres16, recent studies demonstrated that G4s are
enriched
at
promoters17,18
and
related
to
increased
gene
expression19–22. On the other hand, G4s were also located in gene
bodies and related to reduced gene expression by inhibiting elonga-
tion of RNA polymerase23,24. All these previous studies characterized
the biological function of G4s in promoter or promoter-proximal
regions enhancing or reducing gene expression depending on the
relative position of G4s. Nevertheless, the role of G4s in promoter-
distal regulatory elements, such as SE, as well as in chromatin looping
mediating
long-range
enhancer-promoter
interactions
remains
unclear.
The majority of the eukaryotic genome is transcribed into ncRNAs
including microRNAs (miRNAs, 21−25 nucleotides long) and long non-
coding RNAs (lncRNAs, >200 nucleotides long)25. LncRNAs are
important regulators of different biological processes in the nucleus26.
Together with other factors, lncRNAs provide a framework for the
assembly of deﬁned chromatin structures at speciﬁc loci, thereby
modulating gene expression, centromere function, and silencing of
repetitive DNA elements26,27. Although miRNAs are assumed to act
primarily in the cytosol by inhibiting translation28, mature miRNAs
have also been reported in the nuclei of different cells6,29–32. While a
hexanucleotide element has even been reported to direct miRNA
nuclear import33, the function of miRNAs in the cell nucleus has been
sparsely studied. Here we report on microRNA-9 (miR-9), which even
though its nucleotide sequence is highly conserved across species, also
shows high diversity in expression patterns and biological functions
depending on the cellular context34,35. For example, miR-9 has been
reported to target the lncRNA MALAT1 for degradation in the cell
nucleus32. However, it has not been linked to transcription regulation,
chromatin structure nor 3D genome organization. Here we propose a
mechanism of transcriptional regulation of transforming growth fac-
tor beta 1 (TGFB1) responsive genes that requires nuclear miR-9 and
involves G4s and promoter-SE looping.
Results
Mature miR-9 is detected in the cell nucleus and is enriched at
promoters and introns
A phylogenetic tree generated from sequences of mature mouse
miRNAs and a heat map comparing their sequence similarity showed
miR-9 to cluster with miRNAs that have been functionally character-
ized in the cell nucleus, such as miR-29b-3p33, miR-126-5p36 and let-7d-
5p6 (Fig. 1a, top). Sequence alignment between mouse miR-126-5p, miR-
9 and miR-29b-3p showed various nucleotides as conserved in a
sequence stretch reported as nuclear shuttling motif from miR-29b-
3p33 (Fig. 1a, bottom). Accordingly, we refer to the partially conserved
sequence
5´-AKYACCWUUUGRUWA-3´
as
an
expanded
miRNA
nuclear shuttling motif. In addition, we found that the human ortho-
logs of these mature miRNAs, hsa-miR-126-5p, hsa-miR-29B-3p and hsa-
miR-9-5p, also contain the expanded miRNA nuclear shuttling motif
(Supplementary Fig. 1a), demonstrating its conservation across spe-
cies. To conﬁrm the nuclear localization of mature miR-9, we per-
formed expression analysis after cell fractionation using TaqMan
assays speciﬁc for mature miR-9 and total RNA isolated from the
cytosolic and the nuclear fractions of different cells (Fig. 1b and Sup-
plementary Fig. 1b), including mouse lung ﬁbroblasts (MLg and
MFML4), mouse lung epithelial cells (MLE-12), mouse mammary gland
epithelial cells (NMuMG), and primary human lung ﬁbroblasts (hLF).
We were interested in lung cells since miR-9 levels are increased in
hyperproliferative lung diseases. We detected mature miR-9 in the
cytosolic fraction and the nuclear fraction of all cells analyzed. Inter-
estingly, the relative levels of nuclear miR-9 were higher in mouse
ﬁbroblasts as compared to epithelial cells. Further, the nuclear locali-
zation of miR-9 was conﬁrmed by RNA ﬂuorescence in situ hybridiza-
tion (FISH) in MLg cells (Fig. 1c and Supplementary Fig. 1c) and hLF
from control donors (Ctrl hLF) or patients with idiopathic pulmonary
ﬁbrosis (IPF hLF; Supplementary Fig. 1d), a lethal interstitial lung dis-
ease involving TGFB1 signaling37. In MLg cells, we detected miR-9 in
speciﬁc regions of the nuclei, whereas the levels of miR-9 were reduced
after loss-of-function (LOF) experiments using unlabeled miR-9-spe-
ciﬁc antagomiR probes. In Ctrl hLF, the intensity of miR-9 FISH was
higher in the cytosol than in the nucleus, whereas in IPF hLF the
majority of miR-9 was detected in the cell nucleus, pointing to a
translocation mechanism of miR-9 into the cell nucleus potentially
related to IPF. Further, miR-9-LOF in IPF hLF reduced the levels of miR-
9. All these results suggest a function of miR-9 in the cell nucleus. To
investigate the role of miR-9 in the cell nucleus we performed a
sequencing experiment after chromatin isolation by miRNA puriﬁca-
tion (ChIRP-seq) using chromatin from MLg cells and control (Ctrl) or
miR-9-speciﬁc biotinylated antisense oligonucleotides for the pre-
cipitation of endogenous mature miR-9 along with the chromatin
bound to it (Fig. 1d, e and Supplementary Fig. 2a−f). To demonstrate
the speciﬁcity of our ChIRP-seq experiment, we also used a probe
speciﬁc for another miRNA characterized in the cell nucleus (miRNA
lethal 7 d, Mirlet7d, also known as let-7d)8,38, and chromatin from MLE-
12 cells. We detected speciﬁc enrichment of miR-9 at loci without
Article
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-54740-x
Nature Communications|   (2024) 15:10711 
2
Mirlet7d in MLg and MLE-12 cells (Fig. 1d, left, and Supplementary
Fig. 2b, left). Further, genome-wide binding proﬁle analysis of miR-9 in
MLg cells revealed an increase in the number of miR-9 peaks at pro-
moters, transcription termination sides (TTS) and intronic regions
compared to the negative control (Fig. 1d, right), whereas in MLE-12
cells miR-9 was enriched at TTS and intronic regions (Supplementary
Fig. 2b, right). Interestingly, the loci with miR-9 enrichment were
different in MLg and MLE-12 cells (Supplementary Fig. 2c), suggesting
that miR-9 regulates different genes in different cells. The loci of the
putative miR-9 target genes in MLg cells were distributed on all chro-
mosomes (Supplementary Fig. 2e, f). From the miR-9 ChIRP-seq results
we selected putative miR-9 target genes (Zdhhc5, Ncl, Lzts2 and Hdac7)
for further analysis. Visualization of the loci of the putative miR-9 tar-
get genes using the integrative genomic viewer (IGV) (Fig. 1e and

Supplementary Fig. 2d) showed speciﬁc miR-9 enrichment at the pro-
moters, whereas no miR-9 enrichment was detected at the promoter of
the negative control Prap1. These results were conﬁrmed by quanti-
tative PCR (qPCR) after ChIRP using promoter-speciﬁc primers, chro-
matin from MLg cells, and Ctrl or miR-9-speciﬁc biotinylated antisense
oligonucleotides (Fig. 1f). Taken together, our results demonstrate that
mature miR-9 is present in the cell nucleus and directly binds to pro-
moters of putative miR-9-target genes, suggesting a potential role in
transcription regulation.
MiR-9 is required for H3K4me3 broad domains at promoters,
basal transcriptional activity, and G-quadruplex formation
To further investigate a potential role of nuclear miR-9 in transcription
regulation, we performed a sequencing experiment following Cleavage
Under Targets and Tagmentation (CUT&Tag) for high-resolution,
genome-wide
proﬁling
of tri-methylated
lysine
4
of histone
3
(H3K4me3) in MLg and MLE-12 cells that were transiently transfected
with Ctrl or miR-9-speciﬁc antagomiR to induce a miR-9-LOF (Fig. 2a−e,
Supplementary Fig. 3a−d). Peak distribution analysis of the H3K4me3
CUT&Tag showed that 60.8% (P = 0.01) of the H3K4me3 broad domains
in Ctrl transfected MLg cells were enriched with miR-9 (Fig. 2a), whereas
63% (P < 0.01) of the H3K4me3 broad domains were enriched with miR-9
in Ctrl transfected MLE-12 cells (Supplementary Fig. 3b). Interestingly,
H3K4me3 levels at broad domains were signiﬁcantly reduced in MLg
cells from a median of 1.6 RPKM (IQR = 3.3) in Ctrl transfected cells to a
median of 0.8 RPKM (IQR = 1.8; P = 0.002) following miR-9-LOF (Fig. 2b,
top), whereas the effects of miR-9-LOF in MLE-12 cells were not sig-
niﬁcant (Fig. 2b, bottom). In addition, we observed that the loci of the
H3K4me3 broad domains with miR-9 enrichment were different in MLg
and MLE-12 cells (Supplementary Fig. 3c, d), conﬁrming that miR-9
regulates different genes in these two cell lines. Due to these results and
the higher levels of nuclear miR-9 (Fig. 1b), we focused on MLg cells.
Further peak distribution analysis showed that H3K4me3 broad
domains were reduced from 27.6% in Ctrl transfected MLg cells to 22.1%
after miR-9-LOF, whereas medium and narrow H3K4me3 domains
increased (Fig. 2c). Interestingly, the shift from H3K4me3 broad
domains to medium and narrow domains following miR-9-LOF was
signiﬁcant at promoters but not at gene body and intergenic regions
(Fig. 2d). However, enrichment plots showed that H3K4me3 levels were
reduced following miR-9-LOF in H3K4me3 broad and medium domains
at promoter, gene body and intergenic regions (Fig. 2e). The reduction
of H3K4me3 levels after miR-9-LOF was conﬁrmed by confocal micro-
scopy after H3K4me3-speciﬁc immunostaining in Ctrl- and miR-9-
antagomiR transfected MLg cells (Supplementary Fig. 3e).
Since broad domains of H3K4me3 have been associated with
increased transcription elongation39, we analyzed the transcriptome of
MLg cells after miR-9-LOF by total RNA sequencing (RNA-seq, Fig. 2f−h,
Supplementary Fig. 3f). Remarkably, from the transcripts that were
signiﬁcantly affected after miR-9-LOF (n = 3320), only a minority
(n = 881; 26.5%) showed increased expression after miR-9-LOF, whereas
73.5% (n = 2439) showed reduced expression with a median of 1.03
log2 RPKM and an interquartile range (IQR) of 1.51 log2 RPKM
(P = 5.34E-36), when compared to 1.50 log2 RPKM (IQR = 1.81 log2
RPKM) in Ctrl antagomiR transfected cells (Fig. 2g, top). The genes
coding for the transcripts signiﬁcantly affected by miR-9-LOF will be
further referred to as miR-9 target genes. We also observed genes
coding for transcripts that were not signiﬁcantly affected by miR-9-LOF
(Fig. 2g, bottom, non-targets). Remarkably, the most signiﬁcant tran-
scription reducing effect after miR-9-LOF was observed in those tran-
scripts whose promoter or gene body were embedded within
H3K4me3 broad domains (Fig. 2h).
Our results indicate that miR-9 is required for the basal tran-
scriptional activity of its target genes. This interpretation was sup-
ported by sequencing subsequent to chromatin immunoprecipitation
(ChIP-seq) in mouse embryonic ﬁbroblasts (MEF) using antibodies
speciﬁc to total RNA polymerase II (Pol II) and serine 5 phosphorylated
Pol II (Pol II S5p5,) showing transcription initiation. Heat maps repre-
senting the results of the miR-9 ChIRP-seq (Fig. 3a), Pol II and Pol II S5p
ChIP-seq (Fig. 3b) revealed that Pol II and Poll II S5p were enriched at
the transcription start sites (TSS) of the miR-9 target genes. Moreover,
genome-wide precision nuclear run-on assay (PRO-seq)40 and global
run-on sequencing (GRO-seq)41, both in MEF, showed nascent RNAs at
the TSS of the miR-9 target genes demonstrating their basal tran-
scriptional activity (Fig. 3c). Correlating with these results, we also
observed at the TSS of the miR-9 target genes increased chromatin
accessibility and increased H3K4me3 levels by assay for transposase-
accessible chromatin with sequencing (ATAC-seq) and ChIP-seq,
respectively (Fig. 3d). To gain further insight into these results, we
performed a motif search analysis of the miR-9 target genes and
identiﬁed signiﬁcant enrichment of nucleotide motifs with high G
content (Fig. 3e), known to favor the formation of G44,15. Remarkably,
we found similar motifs signiﬁcantly enriched in loci that form G4 as
determined by G4 CUT&Tag. Further, as G4 has been shown to
cooperate with transcription factors at gene promoters20,21, we ana-
lyzed publicly available NGS data generated using different methods
for the assessment of G4 formation (Fig. 3f). On one hand, we analyzed
ChIP-seq data generated using an artiﬁcial 6.7 kDa G4 probe (G4P)
protein, which binds G4s with high afﬁnity and speciﬁcity18. On the
other hand, we analyzed NGS data generated by G4access, which is an
antibody-independent method relying on moderate nuclease diges-
tion of chromatinized DNA21. Remarkably, we found by both approa-
ches enrichment of G4 at the TSS of miR-9 target genes (Fig. 3f). Our
results demonstrate that the TSS of miR-9 target genes show (1)
reduced nucleosome density, (2) increased levels of the euchromatin
histone mark H3K4me3, (3) enrichment of G4, miR-9 and transcription
initiating Poll II S5p, and (4) nascent RNAs.
To demonstrate that miR-9 is required for G4 formation, we ana-
lyzed by CUT&Tag using G4-speciﬁc antibodies chromatin from MLg

introns. a Phylogenetic tree (left) and heat map (right) generated using sequences
of indicated mature mouse miRNAs. Numbers, distance score. Bottom, sequence
alignment of indicated mature mouse miRNAs highlighting the published miR-126

(green square) using IUPAC nucleotide code. Pink letters, conserved among all
sequences; golden, conserved in at least 2 sequences; blue, conserved type of base
(either purine or pyrimidine). b Mature miR-9-speciﬁc TaqMan assay following
cellular fractionation of indicated cell lines. c Fluorescence microscopy of MLg cells
after RNA FISH conﬁrmed nuclear localization of endogenous miR-9. Cells were
transiently transfected with control (top) or miR-9-speciﬁc antagomiR probes
(bottom) to induce a miR-9 loss-of-function (LOF). Representative images from
three independent experiments. Squares are shown at higher magniﬁcation. DAPI,
nucleus. Scale bars, 10 µm. d Left, enrichment plot after miR-9-, or Mirlet7d-speciﬁc
ChIRP-seq in MLg cells. Mirlet7d-speciﬁc probe was used as negative control.

miR-9 peaks by ChIRP-seq in MLg cells in different genomic regions and repre-
sented as Log2 ratios. Pro, promoters; TTS, transcription termination sites; Intron,
intronic regions; 3´UTR, 3´untraslated regions; Interg, intergenic regions.

genomic coordinates. Arrows, direction of genes; blue boxes, exons; red line,

target genes by ChIRP using chromatin from MLg cells and control (Ctrl) or miR-9-
speciﬁc biotinylated antisense oligonucleotides. In all bar plots data are presented
as means; error bars, s.e.m (n = 3 biologically independent experiments); asterisks,

and MLE-12 cells transiently transfected with Ctrl or miR-9-speciﬁc
antagomiR (Fig. 3g and Supplementary Fig. 4a−c). Analysis of the G4
CUT&Tag data without or with ﬁltering based on G4Hunter scores42
showed genome-wide reduction of G4s after miR-9-LOF in MLg and
MLE-12 cells (Fig. 3g and Supplementary Fig. 4b, both left), thereby
demonstrating the requirement of miR-9 for G4 formation in both cell
lines. However, correlating with the levels of nuclear miR-9 in both cell
lines (Fig. 1b), the reducing effect of miR-9-LOF on G4 levels was more
pronounced in MLg cells at TSS of miR-9 target genes (Fig. 3g and
Supplementary Fig. 4b, both right). Interestingly, the majority of the
loci with G4s were different in MLg and MLE-12 cells (Supplementary
Fig. 4c), supporting that G4s are involved in the regulation of different
genes in both cell lines. To verify the interaction between mature miR-9
and G4s, we performed TaqMan-based miRNA enrichment analysis
following chromatin-RNA immunoprecipitation (Ch-RIP) using G4
speciﬁc antibodies (Fig. 3h). G4s signiﬁcantly bound mature miR-9 and
miR-9-LOF abolished this interaction, whereas G4s did not bind mature
Mirlet7f, a miRNA used as negative control, thereby showing the spe-
ciﬁcity of the interaction between mature miR-9 and G4s. To further
investigate this interaction and identify protein-binding partners of
miR-9 in the nucleus, we performed a high-resolution mass spectro-
metry based proteomic approach after miRNA pulldown (miR-Pd)
using the nuclear fraction of MLg or MLE-12 cells and biotinylated
control miRNA (mirctrl) or miR-9 as baits (Fig. 3i, Supplemenatary
Fig. 4d−f and Source Data ﬁle). Results from three independent
experiments identiﬁed 169 proteins in the nuclear fraction of MLg cells
and 233 proteins in the nuclear fraction of MLE-12 cells that were sig-
niﬁcantly enriched after miR-9-Pd. From the miR-9 binding proteins, 20
proteins in the nuclear fraction of MLg cells and 58 proteins in the
nuclear fraction of MLE-12 cells have been reported to interact with
G4s43. Interestingly, miR-9 did not signiﬁcantly bind AGO1, AGO2, and
MEX3D. However, Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA)44 of the miR-9
binding proteins (Fig. 3j) showed that nuclear miR-9 interacted with
proteins involved in G4s (P = 1.23E-4), Chromatin (P = 1.5E-3), RNA

(<2 kb) H3K4me3 domains that are also enriched with miR-9 or not (no miR-9).

different genomic regions (d) in MLg cells that were transfected as in (b). Square in
